title: Driven H-pile axial capacity in layered sand (Nordlund, FHWA GEC-12 abutment)
source: FHWA GEC-12, Design and Construction of Driven Pile Foundations, Vol 3 (FHWA-NHI-16-064), Appendix D, North Abutment design example, Table D-6 (nominal resistance vs depth)
problem: A bridge abutment is supported on driven HP 12x74 H-piles (box convention: perimeter ~4.06 ft, toe area ~1.03 ft2). The pile head is at the bottom of the footing, 5 ft below grade; the profile below the footing is 20 ft of loose silty sand (phi = 33 deg, gamma = 105 pcf = 16.494 kN/m3), then 25 ft of medium dense sand (phi = 36 deg, gamma = 112 pcf = 17.594 kN/m3), then dense sandy gravel (shaft phi = 36 deg, design-limit toe phi = 40 deg, gamma = 125 pcf = 19.636 kN/m3). Groundwater is 10 ft (3.048 m) below the footing. Compute the nominal axial resistance (Nordlund shaft + toe) for a pile driven 60 ft (18.288 m) below the footing. Because the footing sits in an excavation, model depths FROM the footing bottom (z=0 at the pile head), not the ground surface.

layers.description: L3 dense gravel (shaft phi=36, toe phi=40)

published_result: Table D-6 at D = 60 ft below the footing: shaft resistance Rs = 344.1 kips (1531 kN), toe resistance plateau Rt = 428.1 kips (1904 kN), nominal Rn = 772.2 kips (3435 kN). Shaft at other depths: 137.5 kips @ 35 ft, 250.7 @ 50 ft, 452.5 @ 70 ft.
report_notes: A good report states the H-pile box convention (shaft on box perimeter 2(d+bf), toe on box area d*bf — confirm via make_pile_section: 1.237 m = 4.06 ft, 0.0956 m2 = 1.03 ft2), the footing-bottom datum (layers clipped at the footing line, GWT shifted up accordingly — modeling from grade would add ~5 ft of spurious skin friction), and the separate design-limit toe phi = 40 deg via per-layer toe_friction_angle. Computed vs published: shaft +2.6%, toe -4.6% (module's Meyerhof q_L cap at phi=40 governs at 408.5 kips vs the 428.1 kip plateau), total -1.4% — all within the +/-15% validation tolerance. Report Q_skin/Q_tip separately with sigma_v at the tip, and the FS basis (ASD FS = 2.5 here).

title: Drilled shaft side resistance by GEC-10 rational beta (sand) and rational alpha (clay)
source: FHWA GEC-10, Drilled Shafts: Construction Procedures and Design Methods (FHWA-NHI-18-024), Appendix A design example, Steps 11.5-11.6 (Layer 3 sand beta chain, Layer 4 clay alpha chain, base resistance)
problem: An 8-ft (2.4384 m) diameter drilled shaft for a bridge pier passes through a permanently cased 3 m cover zone (no side resistance) into (a) a 20-ft (6.096 m) sand layer with gamma = 17.9 kN/m3, N60 = 30, (N1)60 = 21, pre-scour (no-scour) effective stress sigma_v_ref = 4645 psf = 222.4 kPa at mid-layer, and (b) a 15-ft (4.572 m) stiff clay layer with gamma = 20.3 kN/m3 and su(UU/UC) = 1750 psf = 83.79 kPa, underlain by a cohesionless bearing layer. Compute nominal side resistance using the GEC-10 rational chains: sand beta = Ko*tan(phi') with phi' from (N1)60, sigma'p = 0.47*pa*N60^0.6, OCR from the no-scour stress; clay alpha = 0.30 + 0.17/(su_CIUC/pa) after the Chen & Kulhawy UC-to-CIUC strength transform.
published_result: Layer 3 sand: phi' = 40 deg, sigma'p = 7654 psf, OCR = 1.65, Ko = 0.49, beta = 0.41, fSN = 936 psf, RSN = 470.7 kips (2094 kN). Layer 4 clay: su(CIUC) = 2057 psf, alpha = 0.47, fSN = 976 psf, RSN = 368.1 kips (1637 kN). Base (N60 = 41 sand): qb = 0.60*N60 tsf = 49.2 ksf, RBN = 2473 kips unreduced.
report_notes: A good report shows the full rational chains as intermediate values: for sand, phi' from (N1)60, sigma'p from N60, OCR computed on the NO-SCOUR sigma'v (sigma_v_ref) while fs uses the scour-adjusted design stress, Ko, then beta (0.413 vs published 0.41, side resistance 470.2 vs 470.7 kips, <0.2%); for clay, the UC-to-CIUC transform (su_test_type='uc') before alpha (0.475 vs 0.47, 367.8 vs 368.1 kips). State the GEC-10 exclusion zones (cased/top cover carries no side resistance; bottom-1D cohesive exclusion) — read them off effective_top_m/effective_bottom_m in side_resistance_by_layer. Note the tip-resistance convention: the module applies the O'Neill-Reese 1.27/Db large-diameter reduction for Db > 1.27 m, so Q_tip is ~52% of the example's unreduced RBN — cite which convention the report adopts. For LRFD factored design, follow with lrfd_capacity/get_resistance_factors.

title: Bridge pier spread footing: Vesic bearing capacity plus Hough granular settlement (FHWA GEC-6)
source: FHWA GEC-6, Shallow Foundations (FHWA-SA-02-054), Appendix B, Example 1 'Interior Bridge Pier on Spread Footing', Step 7 bearing capacity and Tables B1-2/B1-3 Hough settlement
problem: An interior bridge pier bears on a 3.0 m square spread footing embedded Df = 2.3 m in silty sand: phi = 35 deg, c = 0, gamma = 19.6 kN/m3, groundwater 9.1 m below grade. First compute ultimate and allowable bearing capacity (Vesic N and shape factors, FS = 3). Then compute settlement under an applied net pressure q = 240 kPa by the Hough method for the four granular layers below the base: L2 silty sand (H = 2.1 m, mid-depth Z = 1.05 m, sigma'vo = 65.7 kPa, C' = 65), L3a sand (4.7 m, 4.45 m, 132 kPa, C' = 120), L3b sand (3.0 m, 8.3 m, 193 kPa, C' = 102), L4 sand (3.0 m, 11.3 m, 222 kPa, C' = 110), using a 2:1 stress distribution.
published_result: Bearing (B = 3 m): qult = 2553 + 254*B = 3315 kPa, qall (FS = 3) = 1106 kPa, built from Nq = 33.3, Ngamma = 48.0, sq = 1.7, sgamma = 0.6, dq = 1.0 (cohesive overburden), AASHTO Cwq = 1.0 / Cwgamma = 0.9. Settlement (B = 3 m, q = 240 kPa): per-layer 15 + 4 + 1 + 1 = 21 mm total (Table B1-3).
report_notes: A good report tabulates the factor basis: N factors (module Nq = 33.30 / Ngamma = 48.03 match the published Vesic/AASHTO Table 5-1 values exactly) and shape factors (sq = 1.700, sgamma = 0.600, exact), then states the depth-factor and groundwater conventions explicitly — the module's compute() runs ~17.6% above the published 3315 kPa because it applies Vesic depth factors (dq = 1.195) where the example sets dq = 1.0 for cohesive overburden, and uses an effective-unit-weight groundwater model (GW at 9.1 m is below the bearing wedge, so no reduction) instead of the AASHTO Cwgamma = 0.9 factor; both are defensible, cite the one used. The settlement half should show per-layer delta_sigma from the 2:1 distribution, sigma'vo, C', and per-layer settlements (computed 15.4/4.4/1.1/0.6 mm vs published 15/4/1/1; total 21.45 vs 21 mm, within the source's per-layer mm rounding), and check the allowable pressure against the settlement-governed service pressure.

title: MSE wall external stability (LRFD) — FHWA GEC-11 Appendix E, Example E4
source: FHWA GEC-11, 'Design and Construction of Mechanically Stabilized Earth Walls and Reinforced Soil Slopes', Vol 2 (FHWA-NHI-10-025), Appendix E, Example E4 (Tables E4-6.1, E4-6.2, E4-6.3).
problem: A segmental precast-panel MSE wall has design height H = 25.64 ft (7.815 m, including 2 ft embedment) with steel bar-mat reinforcement of length L = 18 ft (5.486 m, = 0.7H). Reinforced fill: gamma = 125 pcf (19.636 kN/m3), phi = 34 deg; retained fill: phi = 30 deg, same gamma; foundation: phi = 30 deg. Level backfill with a live-load traffic surcharge q = 0.25 ksf (11.97 kPa, heq = 2 ft of soil). Factored bearing resistance is 10.50 ksf (502.7 kPa) at the strength limit and 7.50 ksf (359.1 kPa) at service. Check external stability (sliding, limiting eccentricity, bearing) per AASHTO/GEC-11 LRFD Strength I (max and min load-factor combinations) and Service I, with live load excluded from the resisting side for sliding/eccentricity but included for bearing.
published_result: Sliding CDRs: Strength I max = 1.85, Strength I min = 2.08, critical max/min = 1.37 (governs). Eccentricity: eL = 2.87 ft (Strength I max), 3.87 ft (critical) vs limit L/4 = 4.50 ft — OK. Bearing: Strength I max eL = 2.60 ft, B' = 12.79 ft, sigma_v = 6.70 ksf, CDR = 1.57; Service I sigma_v = 4.66 ksf; critical-combination sigma_v = 5.86 ksf (source 'consistent-values' rounding). All checks pass.

computed_result.unit_check: eL_strength_max 0.8748 m = 2.87 ft; eL_critical 1.1809 m = 3.87 ft; e_limit 1.3716 m = 4.50 ft; sigma_v 321.1 kPa = 6.71 ksf (pub 6.70); 222.97 kPa = 4.66 ksf; critical 275.41 kPa = 5.75 ksf vs pub 5.86 (within the source's own rounding note, ~2%)

report_notes: A good report tabulates the unfactored loads (V1, Vs, F1, F2 and moment arms about the toe) first, then shows each AASHTO load-factor combination (Strength I max/min, Service I) explicitly, flagging that live load is excluded from the resisting side for sliding/eccentricity but included for bearing. Report CDRs (capacity:demand), not ASD factors of safety — the dispatch method mse_wall reports ASD FOS and is NOT the LRFD path. State the governing combination for each check (critical max/min governs sliding here) and compare eL against the L/4 limit for walls on soil.

title: Cantilever soldier-pile wall — Caltrans Trenching & Shoring Manual Ch. 7, Example 7-1B (simplified method)
source: Caltrans Trenching & Shoring Manual (July 2025), Chapter 7, Example 7-1B: cantilever soldier-pile wall by the AASHTO Simplified method (pp. 133-139).
problem: A 15-ft (4.572 m) cantilever soldier-pile wall retains sand with gamma = 125 pcf (19.636 kN/m3), phi = 35 deg, c = 0, no groundwater. W14x120 soldier piles at 8 ft spacing are set in 2-ft-diameter drilled holes; a 72 psf (3.447 kPa) construction surcharge acts over the retained height (driving side only). Find the earth pressure coefficients and the required embedment (published simplified method: active on the 2-ft hole width, passive on the arching-amplified width f*b = 0.08*phi*b = 5.6 ft, design D = 1.2*D0).
published_result: Rankine Ka = 0.271, Kp = 3.69 (exact module matches). Published simplified soldier-pile results (per pile): toe-moment cubic gives D0 = 12.27 ft, design D = 1.2*D0 = 14.73 ft, zero shear at 6.0 ft below dredge, Mmax = 379.7 kip-ft/pile (= 514.9 kN-m per pile), Vmax = 137.7 kips.

computed_result.unit_check: Ka and Kp match the published 0.271 / 3.69 exactly. Continuous-wall converged embedment 3.45 m = 11.3 ft (x1.2 -> 4.14 m = 13.6 ft) vs the published PER-PILE simplified D0 = 12.27 ft (D = 14.73 ft) — different frameworks, values are close but not equivalent.

report_notes: The report must state the framework explicitly: the published example uses the soldier-pile SIMPLIFIED method (active pressure on the 2-ft hole width, passive on the arching-amplified width f*b = 0.08*phi*b = 5.6 ft, per-pile toe-moment cubic), while the sheet_pile.cantilever_wall solver is a continuous per-metre free-earth-support wall with no soldier-pile arching logic — its embedment is comparable but NOT the same number. Present Ka/Kp with their theory (Rankine, delta = 0), the FOS basis (FOS_passive = 1.0 with the traditional 20% embedment increase here — never both a passive FOS and the 20% rule), and per-pile demands via the tributary spacing when soldier piles are specified. Flag that Caltrans notes Rankine underestimates passive resistance.

title: Two-tier anchored soldier-beam wall (FHWA apparent pressure) — GEC-4 Appendix A, Design Example 1
source: FHWA GEC-4, 'Ground Anchors and Anchored Systems' (FHWA-IF-99-015), Appendix A, Design Example 1: two-tier anchored soldier-beam wall.
problem: A 10 m anchored soldier-beam wall in medium dense silty sand (gamma = 18 kN/m3, phi' = 33 deg, no groundwater) has two anchor levels at 2.5 m and 6.25 m below the top (H1 = 2.5 m, H2 = H3 = 3.75 m). Soldier beams are spaced 2.5 m horizontally and anchors are inclined 15 deg. A traffic surcharge qs = 11 kPa (0.6 m of soil) acts at the surface. Compute the FHWA apparent-pressure trapezoid ordinate pe, the surcharge pressure ps, the tributary horizontal anchor loads, the subgrade reaction, the hinge-method moments, and the inclined anchor design loads.
published_result: Ka = 0.295; apparent-pressure ordinate pe = 43.6 kN/m2; surcharge ps = 3.2 kPa; tributary horizontal anchor loads TH1 = 168 kN/m, TH2 = 172 kN/m; subgrade reaction R = 37 kN/m; anchor design loads DL1 = 435 kN, DL2 = 445 kN; hinge moments M2/M3 = 66 kN-m/m, published M1 = 76 kN-m/m (governs).

computed_result.note: All quantities within 2% of published. max_moment uses the compact (13/54)*H1^2*(pe+ps) top-hinge form = 70.4 kN-m/m — a documented CONVENTION vs the published 76 (GEC-4 applies the uniform surcharge over a larger tributary in the top region); the dominant earth-pressure part (13/54)*pe*H1^2 = 65.6 is exact.

report_notes: A good report draws the trapezoidal apparent-pressure diagram (2/3-H1 top transition, 2/3-Hn+1 bottom transition), shows pe = 0.65*Ka*gamma*H^2 / (H - H1/3 - Hn+1/3) with the Ka basis, and tabulates the tributary calculation for each anchor (TH), the subgrade reaction R, and the design loads DL = TH * spacing / cos(inclination). Note the top-hinge moment convention: the compact (13/54)H1^2(pe+ps) form gives 70.4 vs the published 76 kN-m/m due to the surcharge-tributary treatment — state which was used. Apparent-pressure envelopes are for anchor loads and wall moments; embedment comes from a separate toe analysis.

title: PVD / wick-drain design (Barron-Hansbo radial consolidation) — FHWA GEC-13 Ch. 2 example
source: FHWA GEC-13, 'Ground Modification Methods Reference Manual', Vol 1 (FHWA-NHI-16-027), Chapter 2, Section 4.4, PVD design example (t90 vs drain spacing).
problem: A 20-ft (6.096 m) normally consolidated clay layer (with sand lenses) over rock is to be consolidated with prefabricated vertical drains in a triangular pattern (de = 1.05*s). Properties: cv = 0.1 ft2/day (3.391 m2/yr), ch = 2*cv = 0.2 ft2/day (6.782 m2/yr); drain equivalent diameter dw = 2.5 in (0.0635 m); ideal drains (no smear, no well resistance). Find the time to reach U = 90% radial consolidation for drain spacings of 8 ft (2.438 m) and 10 ft (3.048 m).
published_result: t90 approximately 300 days at s = 8 ft and approximately 500 days at s = 10 ft ('on the order of'), by radial-only ideal Barron-Hansbo consolidation. Module reproduces t90 = 299 days (8 ft) and 503 days (10 ft) — essentially exact.

computed_result.note: Ur (radial) hits 90% at exactly the published times, confirming radial-only Barron-Hansbo with F(n) = ln(n) - 0.75, de = 1.05*s. The published example is RADIAL-ONLY: the module's combined U_total = 1-(1-Uv)(1-Ur) adds ~31-40% vertical consolidation (20-ft clay over rock, single drainage) and exceeds 90% at those times — quote Ur_percent against the published t90, not U_total_percent.

report_notes: The report should show the unit cell explicitly (de = 1.05*s triangular, n = de/dw, F(n) = ln(n) - 0.75 for ideal drains) and the Barron equation t = (de^2/(8*ch))*F(n)*ln(1/(1-U)), then present t90 vs spacing as a small table or curve so the owner can trade spacing against schedule. State the drainage convention used (radial-only here, matching the source) and whether vertical consolidation, smear (ds/dw), and well resistance were included — these are the assumptions reviewers check first. Carry units carefully: ch in m2/yr with time in years.

title: Stone-column settlement improvement (Priebe factor) — FHWA GEC-13 Ch. 5, Example Problem 2
source: FHWA GEC-13, 'Ground Modification Methods Reference Manual', Vol 1 (FHWA-NHI-16-027), Chapter 5, Section 4.3.2, Example Problem 2 with Figure 5-27 (settlement-improvement ratio chart, after Wallays et al. 1983).
problem: A 15-ft embankment (q = 125 pcf * 15 ft = 1,875 psf) loads 50 ft of soft clay (gamma_sat = 120 pcf, WT at surface, Cc = 0.2, eo = 0.6, po at mid-depth = 1,440 psf) over dense sand. Unimproved consolidation settlement is 27 in. Stone columns of d = 3.0 ft at s = 5.7 ft spacing give an area ratio A/Ac = (s/d)^2 = 3.6, i.e. as = Ac/A = 0.277. Estimate the settlement-improvement ratio and the improved settlement (published chart read: ratio = 2.7, improved S = 27/2.7 = 10 in).
published_result: Settlement-improvement ratio = 2.7 (chart read from Figure 5-27); improved settlement = 27 in / 2.7 = 10 in (unimproved S = 27 in reproduced exactly by the consolidation closed form Cc/(1+eo)*H*log10((po+dq)/po)).

computed_result.improved_settlement_range_in: 27/3.06 = 8.8 in (module default phi_column = 42.5) to 27/2.71 = 10.0 in (phi_column = 39, matching the chart)
computed_result.note: The published 2.7 is a chart read; the Priebe (1995) formula result depends on the area-ratio convention and phi_column. At the source's as = 0.277: n0 = 3.06 with the default phi_column = 42.5 (+13%), and n0 = 2.71 at phi_column = 39 (matches the chart). Both bracket the published improved settlement of 10 in from below — within chart-read latitude.

report_notes: The report should state the area-ratio convention up front (as = Ac/A = 1/(s/d)^2 here vs the geometric triangular-tributary value — they differ by ~10%) and the assumed column friction angle, since n0 is sensitive to both; presenting n0 for two or three phi_column values honestly reflects the chart-read latitude of the source. Pair the improvement factor with the unimproved consolidation settlement calculation (settlement module) so the improved settlement S/n0 is traceable end-to-end. Note this is the Priebe BASIC factor n0 (no depth or compressibility corrections).

title: Caltrans Trenching & Shoring Manual Ch. 10 — Ordinary Method of Slices and Simplified Bishop on a specified toe circle
source: California Department of Transportation (Caltrans), California Trenching & Shoring Manual, July 2025 edition, Chapter 10, Examples 10-3 (Ordinary Method of Slices) and 10-4 (Simplified Bishop), pp. 240-243.
problem: A homogeneous slope rises at 4V:3H from a toe at (0, 0) m to a crest at (18.288, 24.384) m (60 ft run, 80 ft rise), with flat ground beyond the crest and left of the toe. Soil: gamma = 18.065 kN/m3 (115 pcf), phi' = 30 deg, c' = 9.576 kPa (200 psf), no groundwater. Compute the factor of safety on a SPECIFIED (pinned, not searched) toe circle centered directly above the toe at (0, 18.288) m with radius 18.288 m (60 ft), by the Ordinary Method of Slices (Fellenius) and by Simplified Bishop. The circle is tangent to the flat ground at the toe, so the analysis radius is nudged to 18.2885 m to give a proper 2-point ground intersection. Published: OMS FS = 0.87; Bishop hand-iterated to '~0.90' (the source stopped one iteration short — the true fixed point of the source's own 6-slice table is 0.96).
published_result: OMS/Fellenius FS = 0.87 (using the source's hand arc length L = 113.55 ft); Simplified Bishop declared '~0.90' after two hand iterations, but the converged fixed point of the source's own slice table is FS = 0.96.

computed_result.tolerance: Fellenius 0.833 vs published 0.87 within +/-0.05 (the spread is the source's over-stated hand arc length in the c'*L term plus the module's geometric exit at x~18.3 m vs the source's 17.56 m); Bishop 0.971 matches the CONVERGED fixed point 0.96 within 1.2% — the published 0.90 is under-iterated, not a module error.

report_notes: A good report states the method basis explicitly (OMS neglects interslice forces and is conservative; Bishop satisfies vertical equilibrium and must be ITERATED to the FS = FSa fixed point) and presents the per-slice table (alpha, W, W sin, W cos) so the geometry reconstruction is checkable against the source. Because this is a pinned-circle check, the report must say so — no search story is claimed — and should show the circle, entry/exit points, and slope face on a section figure. A method-comparison table (compare_methods=true) is the right exhibit: it shows the OMS-vs-rigorous spread (~0.83 vs ~0.96) and flags that FS < 1 either way. When a published value disagrees, report both and explain the discrepancy (here: under-iterated source Bishop; hand arc-length convention in the cohesion term).

title: Duncan (2000) LASH Terminal underwater slope — FOSM reliability with a depth-varying correlated su gradient
source: Duncan, J.M. (2000). 'Factors of Safety and Reliability in Geotechnical Engineering.' ASCE Journal of Geotechnical and Geoenvironmental Engineering, 126(4), pp. 307-316 — LASH Terminal underwater slope; also reproduced as Rocscience Slide2 Verification Problem #29 (manual pp. 121-122, Table 29.2).
problem: A 100-ft-high underwater excavated slope in San Francisco Bay Mud at the LASH terminal, fully submerged (water surface at elevation 0). Section (m, converted from the labeled figure in feet): mudline runs from (-8.53, -12.19) down the excavated face to a base bench at elevation -36.58 m between x = 21.64 and 42.06, then back up natural ground to a crest at elevation +6.71 m. Undrained strength increases linearly with depth: su = 4.788 kPa (100 psf) at elevation -6.096 m (el -20 ft), gradient b = 1.539 kPa/m (9.8 psf/ft), floored at 0.718 kPa; gamma_sat = 15.709 kN/m3 (100 pcf). The observed/critical noncircular failure surface (polyline from (42.06, -36.58) to (106.68, -2.44)) is analyzed with Spencer's method. Probabilistic inputs (Duncan Table): unit-weight std 3.3 pcf (COV 0.033, applied to gamma_sat — buoyancy amplifies it) and su-gradient std 1.2 psf/ft (0.1885 kPa/m), the (a, b) pair treated as ONE coherent random law across all sub-layers. An alternative characterization (Duncan's full-profile su COV = 0.16 on intercept AND gradient, perfectly correlated) reproduces his COV_F ~ 0.16 / Pf ~ 18%. Published anchors: F = 1.17, COV_F ~= 0.16, Pf ~= 18% (Duncan closed-form Taylor series); Slide2 per-method simulated Pf 13-18%.
published_result: Duncan (2000) closed-form Taylor series: F = 1.17, COV_F ~= 0.16, Pf ~= 18%. Slide2 #29 per-method simulated Pf: 13-18%.

computed_result.alt_full_profile_su_cov_0p16: COV_F = 0.1599, pf = 0.1527 — reproduces Duncan's COV_F ~ 0.16 / Pf ~ 18% at his F = 1.17 (run the same call with variables = {bay_mud_su: {law: linear_su, a: {mean: 4.788, cov: 0.16}, b: {mean: 1.53945, cov: 0.16}, rho_ab: 1.0, datum_z: -6.096, z_ref: mid, su_min: 0.7182}})
computed_result.tolerance: Deterministic Spencer 1.192 vs Duncan 1.17 within 2%. With the STATED input stds: COV_F 0.133, Pf ~10-13% — inside the Slide2 per-method band.
computed_result.key_physics: gamma_sat (not dry gamma) must be the varied unit weight on a submerged slope: buoyancy amplifies its COV 0.033 into ~0.088 on the buoyant weight, contributing ~42% of Var[FOS].

report_notes: A slope reliability report should present the deterministic FOS at mean values first, then the propagation: per-variable variance contributions (a tornado-style table from variable_variance_pct), COV_F, the lognormal reliability index, and Pf — stating that the true answer is a distribution, not the point FOS. It must document HOW each uncertainty was characterized (regression-slope std vs full-profile COV changes Pf from ~11% to ~18% here) and why gamma_sat is the varied unit weight on a submerged slope (buoyancy amplification). Show the analyzed slip surface on the section with the su(z) profile and its uncertainty band, and cross-check FOSM against Monte Carlo when practical.

title: Slide2 #36 (Li & Lumb 1987 / Hassan & Wolff 1999) — critical-surface search plus FOSM/Monte Carlo reliability index
source: Rocscience Slide2 Verification Manual, Verification Problem #36, pp. 137-139; after Li, K.S. & Lumb, P. (1987), Canadian Geotechnical Journal 24(4), and Hassan, A.M. & Wolff, T.F. (1999), ASCE JGGE 125(4), Table 36.1/36.2.
problem: A homogeneous 10-m-high 1H:1V slope: ground surface (0, 5), (5, 5), (15, 15), (20, 15) m with the soil extending down to elevation 0. Mean properties: c' = 18 kPa, phi' = 30 deg, gamma = 18 kN/m3, pore-pressure ratio ru = 0.20 (drained analysis). Random variables (normal): c' std 3.6 kPa (COV 0.20), phi std 3.0 deg (COV 0.10), gamma std 0.9 kN/m3 (COV 0.05), ru std 0.02. Task: find the deterministic critical circle by Simplified Bishop, then compute the lognormal reliability index of the FOS on that surface. Published (Table 36.2): Slide2 Bishop deterministic-minimum FOS 1.340 with RI_LN 2.482 (overall 1.350/2.393); Hassan & Wolff FOS 1.334, RI_LN 2.336.
published_result: Slide2 Bishop: deterministic-minimum FOS 1.340, RI_LN 2.482 (overall-slope FOS 1.350, RI 2.393). Hassan & Wolff (1999): FOS 1.334, RI_LN 2.336.

computed_result.tolerance: Searched Bishop FOS 1.325 within 0.7% of Hassan-Wolff 1.334 and 1.1% of Slide2 1.340. Module RI_LN 2.244 within ~4% of Hassan-Wolff 2.336; the FOSM COV_F 0.1225 evaluated at the published FOS 1.334 gives RI 2.30, within 1.6%. Monte Carlo confirms FOSM (COV 0.1224 vs 0.1225).

report_notes: The report should tell the search story: search strategy (entry-exit circular grid), number of surfaces evaluated (3,810), seed for reproducibility, and the critical circle's center/radius/entry-exit — with a figure of the critical surface and FOS contours. The reliability annex then fixes that surface and presents COV_F, RI (state the lognormal-FOS assumption explicitly), Pf, and the variance breakdown showing the design is c'-dominated (~70%) — which directs any site-investigation spend toward cohesion. Cross-validate FOSM with Monte Carlo and, when comparing to published RIs, note whether they are on the deterministic-minimum or overall-slope surface.

title: Slide2 #39 (Tandjiria 2002) — sand embankment on soft clay: unreinforced Spencer FOS and required geosynthetic force for FS = 1.35
source: Rocscience Slide2 Verification Manual, Verification Problem #39, pp. 147-150; after Tandjiria, V., Teh, C.I. & Low, B.K. (2002), sand-fill case (Table 39.2).
problem: A 6-m-high sand embankment on a 3-m soft-clay foundation: ground surface (0, 9), (10, 9), (20, 3), (30, 3) m — crest at elevation 9 m for x in [0, 10], a 2H:1V face down to (20, 3), then a bench at elevation 3 m. Fill (el 3-9 m): c' = 0, phi' = 37 deg, gamma = 17 kN/m3, drained. Soft clay (el 0-3 m): cu = 20 kPa, phi = 0, gamma = 20 kN/m3, undrained. No groundwater modeled. Tasks: (1) Spencer FOS on the critical circle without reinforcement (pinned circle from an entry-exit search, center (15.19, 10.24) m, R = 9.94 m); (2) the horizontal geosynthetic force (single layer at the fill base, elevation 3 m) required to raise that surface to FS = 1.35, found by bisection on T_allow. Published: unreinforced Spencer circular FS 1.209 (Slide2) / 1.219 (Tandjiria); required force 44 (circular) to 56 (noncircular) kN/m.
published_result: Unreinforced sand-fill Spencer FOS: 1.209 circular / 1.188 noncircular (Slide2); 1.219 / 1.192 (Tandjiria 2002). Geosynthetic force to reach FS = 1.35: 44-45 kN/m circular, 56 kN/m noncircular.

computed_result.tolerance: Unreinforced 1.180 within 2.4% of Slide2 1.209. The solved force 55.5 kN/m brackets the published 44-56 kN/m band; the reinforcement force is highly critical-surface-sensitive, so the circular/noncircular split differs from Slide2's (the manual itself attributes such spreads to search-method differences).

report_notes: The report should present the unreinforced baseline first (method, critical surface through the soft clay, FOS vs the 1.35 requirement), then the reinforcement design as a demand curve: FOS vs T_allow from the bisection, with the required force where it crosses the target. State the reinforcement model (active horizontal force at the fill-base elevation where the layer crosses the slip surface) and flag that required force is strongly critical-surface-dependent — re-search the critical surface WITH reinforcement as a check, since the reinforced critical surface can migrate. A sensitivity note on cu of the soft clay and on circular vs noncircular surfaces closes the loop against the published 44-56 kN/m spread.

title: Pilarcitos Dam (1969 upstream failure) — rapid-drawdown critical-surface search, three total-stress methods
source: Rocscience Slide2 Verification Manual, Verification Problem #97; Duncan, J.M., Wright, S.G. & Wong, K.S. (1990), 'Slope stability during rapid drawdown,' H. Bolton Seed Memorial Symposium, Vol. 2; also GEO-SLOPE rapid-drawdown Example 3. Pilarcitos Dam, California — a real homogeneous rolled earth-fill dam whose upstream slope failed during the Oct-Nov 1969 reservoir drawdown from El. 692 to El. 657 ft.
problem: Pilarcitos Dam: homogeneous rolled earth fill, height 78 ft (toe at relative el 0, crest el 78 ft = 23.77 m). Fill properties (published): gamma = 21.207 kN/m3 (135 pcf); effective-stress envelope c' = 0, phi' = 45 deg; total-stress R-envelope (undrained response of the low-permeability fill) R_c = 2.873 kPa (60 psf), R_phi = 23 deg. Reservoir drawn down from El. 72 ft (21.946 m) to El. 37 ft (11.278 m) above the toe. The upstream slope angle is NOT published; a representative 2.5H:1V face is used ((0,0) to (59.44, 23.77) m, crest to (83.82, 23.77) m; fill base at -2.44 m), and the three-method ORDERING is slope-invariant (holds at 2:1, 2.5:1, 3:1). Search for the critical circular surface under each total-stress rapid-drawdown method: USACE Corps 2-stage, Lowe-Karafiath 2-stage, and Duncan-Wright-Wong 3-stage. Published (Slide2 #97): Corps 0.823, Lowe-Karafiath 1.047, DWW 1.043 (DWW 1990: 0.82 / 1.05 / 1.05).
published_result: Slide2 #97: Corps 2-stage FOS 0.823, Lowe-Karafiath 1.047, Duncan-Wright-Wong 3-stage 1.043 (Duncan, Wright & Wong 1990: 0.82 / 1.05 / 1.05). The dam actually failed on the upstream slope during the 1969 drawdown.

computed_result.tolerance: Corps 2-stage 0.787 lands near the published 0.823 (within ~0.05) and BELOW 1.0, reproducing the observed failure on the representative section. The three-method ordering Corps < DWW <= LK (with DWW ~= LK, the stage-3 drained check barely firing) matches the published signature and is slope-invariant. LK/DWW magnitudes overshoot the published ~1.05 (~1.35 here) for two documented, un-tuned reasons: the upstream slope geometry is assumed (not published), and the very steep published phi' = 45 deg inflates the Kc-interpolated strength gain (Kf = 5.83); on the moderate-phi' Slide2 #98 dam the same code matched published spacing to ~10%.

report_notes: A rapid-drawdown report must state the staged method basis (Corps 2-stage min(R, effective) envelope vs Kc-interpolated Lowe-Karafiath vs DWW 3-stage with drained substitution) and present all three as a method-comparison table, since they can span FS < 1 to FS > 1 on the same section — the Corps method is the conservative screening value. Document the drawdown range, the stage-1 phreatic assumption (flat full-pool here, the conservative no-through-seepage bound), the R-envelope source, and the search story (center-grid extent, surfaces evaluated, critical circle). When geometry is reconstructed rather than published, say so explicitly and demonstrate which conclusions are geometry-invariant (the method ordering) versus geometry-dependent (the magnitudes); a case history that failed in service should be shown to be predicted unstable by the design method.

title: 1-D coupled Biot consolidation column (Itasca FLAC verification V-023) via fem2d monolithic u-p solve
source: Itasca FLAC2D/FLAC3D Verification Manual, 'One-Dimensional Consolidation' (Terzaghi/Biot column)
problem: A laterally confined saturated soil column, H = 20 m deep and 2 m wide, has a fixed impermeable base and a drained top surface (p = 0). A surface load pz = 1e5 Pa (100 kPa) is applied instantaneously. Elastic bulk modulus K = 5e8 Pa and shear modulus G = 2e8 Pa (equivalent E = 5.294e8 Pa = 5.294e5 kPa, nu = 0.3235); Biot coefficient alpha = 1.0, Biot modulus M = 4e9 Pa (= 4e6 kPa); mobility coefficient k = 1e-10 m^2/(Pa.s) (= 1e-7 m^2/(kPa.s)). Compute the undrained pore-pressure response p0, the consolidation transient, and the final drained settlement. Closed-form targets: p0 = alpha*M/(K + 4G/3 + alpha^2*M)*pz = 0.839e5 Pa; final settlement w = pz*H/(K + 4G/3) = 2.609 mm; coefficient of consolidation c = k/S = 0.0643 m^2/s.
published_result: Final drained settlement w = pz*H/(K+4G/3) = 2.609 mm (Itasca quotes 2.61 mm); load-induced undrained pore pressure p0 = 0.839e5 Pa (the value consistent with the stated M = 4e9 Pa); pore pressure dissipates per the Terzaghi/Biot solution with c = 0.0643 m^2/s, FLAC matching within <5%.

computed_result.notes: Interior (median) t=0 excess pore pressure = 83.92 kPa vs analytical p0 = 83.9 kPa (<0.1%). The field max of 118.85 kPa is a localized t=0 spatial overshoot in the first pressure nodes below the drained top boundary (known Taylor-Hood undrained-boundary artifact), NOT the physical p0. Final time point corresponds to time factor T = 2.0 (U = 0.98). Tolerance: settlement and p0 within 1%; Terzaghi decay within 5% with theta = 0.5 (Crank-Nicolson).

report_notes: A good report states the unit mapping explicitly (fem2d works in kPa: stresses/moduli Pa/1000; for the monolithic scheme k is the MOBILITY in m^2/(kPa.s) and n_w is the Biot modulus M in kPa), and why the monolithic u-p scheme was chosen (the default staggered split cannot generate load-induced undrained pore pressure). Present the settlement-time curve and pore-pressure dissipation against the closed-form anchors (p0, final drained settlement, c), quoting the interior p0 rather than the boundary-overshoot field max.

title: AASHTO 1993 flexible pavement design — Figure 3.1 printed worked example (SN = 5.0)
source: AASHTO Guide for Design of Pavement Structures (1993), Part II, Figure 3.1 design chart worked example (printed p. II-32)
problem: Design a flexible pavement for W18 = 5,000,000 18-kip ESALs in the design lane, reliability R = 95% (ZR = -1.645), overall standard deviation So = 0.35, effective roadbed resilient modulus MR = 5,000 psi, and design serviceability loss dPSI = 1.9. Layer materials: asphalt concrete EAC = 400,000 psi, granular base EBS = 30,000 psi, granular subbase ESB = 11,000 psi. Find the required structural number SN and a layered section (US customary units).
published_result: Printed chart solution SN = 5.0 (nomograph read-off, ~0.1 resolution; the equation solve gives 4.95-5.0).

computed_result.notes: sn_required = 4.976 vs printed 5.0 — within the 0.1 nomograph read-off tolerance. The module also returns the Figure 3.2 layer split (a1 = 0.42 from Figure 2.5, a2 = 0.1378 and a3 = 0.0784 from the printed regressions, m = 1.0 defaults) and a forward check (w18_capacity >= w18).

report_notes: A good calc report presents the design-equation inputs (W18, ZR from Table 4.1, So, MR, dPSI) with their guide-section citations, the solved SN against the printed nomograph value with the chart-read tolerance stated, and the Figure 3.2 top-down layer split showing each layer's coefficient a_i, drainage m_i, its basis, and the cumulative SN over each foundation. It should close with the forward check (ESAL capacity of the provided section >= design W18) and echo defaulted values (m = 1.0; So outside the recommended flexible 0.40-0.50 range is flagged in notes).

title: AASHTO 1993 rigid pavement design — Figure 3.7 printed worked example (D = 10.0 in)
source: AASHTO Guide for Design of Pavement Structures (1993), Part II, Figure 3.7 rigid design chart worked example (printed pp. II-45/46)
problem: Design a jointed PCC slab for W18 = 5,100,000 18-kip ESALs with effective modulus of subgrade reaction k = 72 pci, concrete elastic modulus Ec = 5,000,000 psi, modulus of rupture S'c = 650 psi, load transfer coefficient J = 3.2, drainage coefficient Cd = 1.0, overall standard deviation So = 0.29, reliability R = 95%, terminal serviceability pt = 2.5, and design serviceability loss dPSI = 4.5 - 2.5 = 1.7. Find the required slab thickness D (US customary units).
published_result: Printed chart solution D = 10.0 in (nomograph, nearest half-inch; the equation solve gives ~9.7-10.0 in).

computed_result.notes: d_required = 9.72 in, rounded up to the 10.0-in provided slab — matches the printed 10.0 in at the guide's half-inch nomograph resolution.

report_notes: A good calc report tabulates every rigid design-equation input (W18, ZR, So, S'c, Ec, J, Cd, k, dPSI, pt) with its source (Table 4.1 for ZR; how k was obtained — here direct, otherwise MR/19.4 or the Section 3.2 composite-k worksheet iterated with D), presents the unrounded required D beside the rounded provided slab, and states the half-inch chart-read tolerance against the printed solution. It should include the forward check (capacity at the provided D exceeds design W18) and note that pt enters both the serviceability term and the strength-term exponent.

title: UFC 3-250-01 alternative method — AASHTO vs UFC flexible comparison and Table G-1 mixed-traffic equivalency
source: UFC 3-250-01 (2016), Pavement Design for Roads and Parking Areas: Section 4-3.3 mixed-traffic procedure with worked example Table G-1 (printed p. 257), Appendix E vehicle curves; AASHTO 1993 Guide for the comparison leg; FHWA-NHI-05-037 Table 5-34 Mr = 2555*CBR^0.64 bridge correlation
problem: (a) Comparison: for 1,000,000 passes of the 18-kip single axle on a subgrade with CBR = 6 (Mr = 2555*6^0.64 = 8,043 psi via the FHWA correlation), base CBR 80 and subbase CBR 30, design the flexible section by BOTH the AASHTO 1993 procedure (R = 95%, So = 0.45, pt = 2.5) and the UFC 3-250-01 CBR cover-curve procedure, and compare totals. (b) Mixed traffic (Table G-1 example): traffic of 1,000,000 passes of the 18-kip ESAL (E-1), 100,000 passes of a 5-axle truck (E-28), 500,000 passes of a 3-axle truck (E-26), and 20,000,000 passenger cars (E-2) at representative subgrade CBR = 3; find the controlling vehicle and the total equivalent 18-kip passes.
published_result: Table G-1 printed worked example: the 18-kip ESAL (E-1) controls at 16.4 in required thickness; light vehicles whose required thickness falls below the controlling thickness contribute unlimited allowable passes (negligible equivalent contribution); total equivalent 18-kip passes = 1,395,400. The comparison leg has no single printed answer — differing AASHTO vs UFC sections are expected because the damage models differ.

computed_result.notes: Comparison: same physical 18-kip pass count on both sides (AASHTO LEF(18k single) = 1.0 by definition); reliability exists only in AASHTO (R = 95%, So = 0.45) — the UFC procedure is deterministic; CBR<->Mr bridged via Mr = 2555*CBR^0.64. Mixed traffic: 1,316,228 computed vs printed 1,395,400 (-5.7%, within the stated 6% inversion tolerance; the near-flat E-28 5-axle curve inversion dominates the residual); E-26 and E-2 drop out as unlimited.

report_notes: A good report keeps the two guides' results side by side and explicitly states the cross-guide assumptions the module echoes in notes: the shared 18-kip traffic basis, the CBR-to-Mr correlation used for the bridge, and that reliability is an AASHTO-only concept (UFC is deterministic) — so a thinner/thicker UFC section is a damage-model difference, not an error. For the mixed-traffic leg, reproduce the Table G-1 worksheet (per-vehicle required thickness at the representative CBR, controlling vehicle, allowable passes at the controlling thickness, equivalent-pass ratios), note which light vehicles drop out as unlimited, and state the curve-inversion tolerance (within ~6% of the printed total); the resulting equivalent E-1 passes then feed flexible_pavement_design_ufc at the real site CBR.

title: Ultimate bearing capacity of a strip footing, dry and with GWT at footing base (FHWA Soils & Foundations Example 8-1)
source: FHWA NHI-06-089, Soils and Foundations Reference Manual Vol II (Dec 2006), Chapter 8, Example 8-1, printed p. 8-21 (bearing capacity factors from Table 8-2, printed p. 8-20)
problem: A rigid, rough-based, concentrically loaded strip footing is Bf = 6 ft (1.8288 m) wide and embedded Df = 5 ft (1.524 m). Soil above and below the base is identical: total unit weight 125 pcf (19.636 kN/m3), phi = 20 deg, c = 500 psf (23.94 kPa). Compute the ultimate bearing capacity qult (a) with groundwater well below the footing and (b) with the groundwater table at the footing base (5 ft depth), using the AASHTO (2004) three-term formula qult = c*Nc + gamma*Df*Nq + 0.5*gamma*Bf*Ngamma with Nc = 14.8, Nq = 6.4, Ngamma = 5.4 at phi = 20 deg. Conversions used: 1 psf = 0.04788 kPa, 1 pcf = 0.15709 kN/m3, 1 ft = 0.3048 m.
published_result: Nc = 14.8, Nq = 6.4, Ngamma = 5.4 (Table 8-2 at phi = 20 deg). Dry case: qult = 7,400 + 4,000 + 2,025 = 13,425 psf (642.8 kPa). GWT at footing base (buoyant unit weight 125 - 62.4 = 62.6 pcf in the weight term): qult = 7,400 + 4,000 + 1,014 = 12,414 psf (594.4 kPa).

computed_result.factors: Nc = 14.83, Nq = 6.40, Ngamma = 5.39 - matches Table 8-2 (14.8 / 6.4 / 5.4) to the printed precision (Ngamma = 2(Nq+1)tan(phi) is the same AASHTO/Vesic expression).
computed_result.vs_published: Dry +25.1% (804.2 vs 642.8 kPa); GWT case +27.2% (755.9 vs 594.4 kPa). The entire difference is the Vesic/Hansen DEPTH FACTORS (dc = 1.311, dq = 1.263, dgamma = 1.0 at Df/B = 0.83) that our GEC-6-style analysis applies but the manual's plain Eq. 8-1 omits.
computed_result.depth_factor_reconciliation: Dividing the returned terms by the returned depth factors reproduces the manual: (465.69/1.3113) + (241.8/1.2626) + 96.71 = 643.4 kPa vs printed 642.8 kPa (+0.1%); GWT case (355.2 + 191.5 + 48.4) = 595.1 kPa vs printed 594.4 kPa (+0.1%). The GWT effect itself (weight term halved via gamma' = gamma - 9.81) matches the manual's buoyant-weight treatment.

report_notes: The module reproduces the manual's bearing capacity factors exactly and its three-term assembly to 0.1% once the depth-factor convention is accounted for: FHWA NHI-06-089 Eq. 8-1 is the bare Terzaghi-form equation with NO depth factors, while bearing_capacity_analysis follows AASHTO/GEC-6 practice and applies Vesic depth factors for embedded footings (here +25%). A report should state which convention is used and note that omitting depth factors is conservative. The result echoes dc/dq/dgamma explicitly so the reviewer can reconcile the two conventions line by line, as done here.
source_doc: FHWA NHI-06-089 Soils and Foundations Vol 2.pdf

title: Required length of a precast concrete pile in two clay layers by the alpha-method (FHWA Soils & Foundations Example 9-3)
source: FHWA NHI-06-089, Soils and Foundations Reference Manual Vol II (Dec 2006), Chapter 9, Example 9-3, printed pp. 9-58 to 9-59
problem: A 1 ft x 1 ft (0.3048 m) square precast concrete pile must carry an allowable load of 40 tons (355.9 kN) with FS = 2, i.e. required Qu = 80 tons (711.7 kN). Toe resistance is neglected. Profile: upper clay D1 = 10 ft (3.048 m), gamma = 120 pcf (18.85 kN/m3), c1 = 500 psf (23.94 kPa), phi = 0; below it a stiffer clay, gamma = 130 pcf (20.42 kN/m3), c2 = 1,100 psf (52.67 kPa), phi = 0. Site-specific tests indicate adhesion = cohesion (alpha = 1.0) in BOTH layers. The manual solves for the required penetration D2 into the lower clay (~32 ft) giving a total length of ~42 ft (12.80 m). Here we verify the 42-ft pile by computing its capacity. Conversions: 1 psf = 0.04788 kPa, 1 pcf = 0.15709 kN/m3, 1 ton = 8.896 kN.
published_result: Qu = ca1*Cd1*D1 + ca2*Cd2*D2 with Cd = 4 ft, adhesion = cohesion: 80 tons = 10 tons (upper clay, 89.0 kN) + 2.2 tons/ft x D2, giving D2 ~ 32 ft and total pile length ~ 42 ft. So at 42 ft: Rs1 = 10 tons (89.0 kN), Rs2 = 70 tons (622.7 kN), toe neglected, Qu = 80 tons = 711.7 kN; Qa = 40 tons = 355.9 kN.

computed_result.vs_published: Q_ultimate 535.1 vs 711.7 kN (-24.8%). The gap is an input-assumption difference, not an arithmetic one: the manual's site-specific stipulation is adhesion = cohesion (alpha = 1.0) in both layers, while axial_pile_capacity applies the standard Tomlinson design curve for concrete piles, which gives alpha = 1.0 for the soft clay (upper-layer skin 89.0 kN matches the printed 10 tons EXACTLY) but alpha = 0.642 at cu = 52.7 kPa in the lower clay.
computed_result.reconciliation: Lower-layer skin computed 402.1 kN; dividing out the module's alpha (402.1/0.642 = 626.4 kN) reproduces the printed 622.7 kN within +0.6%. With alpha = 1 in both layers and the toe dropped (the manual neglects 9*cu*At; the module includes it, +44.0 kN) the kernel reproduces the printed 711.7 kN within 0.5%. Perimeter (1.2192 m = 4 ft) and tip area (0.0929 m2 = 1 ft2) match the manual's Cd and At exactly.

report_notes: A capacity report should state the adhesion basis explicitly: the manual uses a site-specific load-test-backed alpha = 1.0, whereas without such data standard practice (and this module) reads alpha from the Tomlinson curves, which is 36% lower in the 1,100-psf clay - a defensible, conservative difference that directly lengthens the required pile. Also state whether toe resistance in clay is counted (the manual neglects it; the module reports it separately as Q_tip so it can be excluded). The reconciliation above shows the module's alpha-method kernel matches the printed arithmetic to <1% once those two assumptions are aligned.
source_doc: FHWA NHI-06-089 Soils and Foundations Vol 2.pdf

title: External stability of a CIP cantilever retaining wall with sloping backfill (FHWA Soils & Foundations Example 10-3)
source: FHWA NHI-06-089, Soils and Foundations Reference Manual Vol II (Dec 2006), Chapter 10, Example 10-3, printed pp. 10-52 to 10-56
problem: Check sliding, eccentricity, and bearing for a CIP cantilever wall: stem height 18 ft with top thickness 1.6 ft and base thickness 2.3 ft (front-face batter), base slab 13.1 ft wide x 2.3 ft thick, toe projection 2.3 ft, heel slab 8.5 ft; total height base-of-footing to top-of-stem 20.3 ft (6.187 m). Backfill: clean fine-to-medium sand, gamma = 115 pcf (18.065 kN/m3), phi = 30 deg, backfill slope beta = 10 deg. Foundation soil: gamma = 120 pcf (18.851 kN/m3), phi_b = 38 deg; base interface friction delta_b = (3/4)phi_b = 28.5 deg (NAVFAC); passive resistance in front of the wall neglected; groundwater well below the base; concrete 150 pcf (23.563 kN/m3). Earth pressure on the vertical plane at the back of the heel with thrust inclined at delta = beta = 10 deg (the manual's Coulomb Ka with delta = beta equals the sloping-backfill Rankine coefficient used here), pressure height H = 21.8 ft including the heel slope rise. Conversions: 1 ft = 0.3048 m, 1 pcf = 0.15709 kN/m3, 1 lb/ft = 0.014594 kN/m, 1 psf = 0.04788 kPa.
published_result: Ka = 0.35 (Coulomb, phi = 30, beta = delta = 10 deg); Pa = 9,564.2 lb/ft (139.6 kN/m) with Ph = 9,418.9 lb/ft and Pv = 1,660.8 lb/ft; total weight W = 28,112.6 lb/ft; FS_sliding = 1.72 (delta_b = 28.5 deg, passive neglected); eccentricity e = 1.0 ft (0.305 m) < B/6 = 2.18 ft; q_max = 3,318.1 psf (158.9 kPa), q_min = 1,227.3 psf (58.8 kPa); FS_bearing = 6.03 using an ASSUMED qult = 20,000 psf.

computed_result.Ka_coulomb_call: 0.3495 vs printed 0.35 (-0.1%); the analysis call's sloped-Rankine coefficient is numerically identical (Coulomb with delta = beta on a vertical plane).
computed_result.vs_published: FS_sliding 1.719 vs 1.72 (-0.1%); Pa 139.4 vs 139.6 kN/m (-0.1%); Pv 24.2 vs 24.2 kN/m (0%); q_toe 161.5 vs 158.9 kPa (+1.6%); q_heel 56.1 vs 58.8 kPa (-4.5%); eccentricity 0.322 vs 0.305 m (+5.6%). The small e/q differences come from the stem-taper orientation: the module places the stem taper on the back face while the manual's wall batters the front face - identical weights (sliding unaffected), slightly different moment arms (~0.8% of the total moment). The module's FOS_bearing (reported 99.9, i.e. capped) computes its own Meyerhof qult from phi_foundation = 38 deg, which is far above the manual's assumed 20,000 psf, so the printed FS_bc = 6.03 is not comparable; q_max vs the assumed qult can be checked by hand (20,000 psf / 3,373 psf computed = 5.9).

report_notes: This is the manual's flagship gravity-wall external-stability check and the module reproduces the governing numbers (Pa, Pv, FS_sliding, bearing pressures) to within 2%. A report should state the earth-pressure plane and inclination convention (vertical plane at the heel back, thrust at delta = beta), the base-interface basis (delta_b = 3/4 phi_b entered directly via delta_base - the sliding_basis echo confirms 28.5 deg with no extra 2/3 factor), and that passive resistance was conservatively neglected. Bearing FOS should be quoted against a properly computed qult for the eccentric, inclined resultant rather than the module's default full-strength Meyerhof value; the manual simply assumes qult = 20 ksf for illustration.
source_doc: FHWA NHI-06-089 Soils and Foundations Vol 2.pdf

title: Factor of safety of a homogeneous fill slope - Taylor chart hand solution vs critical-surface search (FHWA Soils & Foundations Example 6-1)
source: FHWA NHI-06-088, Soils and Foundations Reference Manual Vol I (Dec 2006), Chapter 6, Examples 6-1 and 6-1a, printed pp. 6-30 to 6-32 (ReSSA computer check cited on p. 6-32)
problem: Determine the factor of safety of a 30-ft (9.144 m) high fill slope inclined at 30 deg. Homogeneous fill: total unit weight 120 pcf (18.851 kN/m3), c' = 500 psf (23.94 kPa), phi' = 20 deg; no groundwater. Geometry modeled as level ground at elev 0 from x = -15 m to the toe at (0, 0), slope face rising at 30 deg to the crest at (15.84, 9.144), level crest beyond; soil extends to elev -15 m. Search for the critical circular surface (Bishop simplified). Conversions: 1 ft = 0.3048 m, 1 psf = 0.04788 kPa, 1 pcf = 0.15709 kN/m3.
published_result: Taylor-chart iterative solution (Fc = Fphi): FS ~ 1.9 (converged at Ns = 0.073, phi_d = 10.8 deg, back-computed H = 30.04 ft). Alternate graphical Fc-vs-Fphi intersection (Example 6-1a): FS = 1.95. The same problem run in the ReSSA computer program (Appendix D) gives FS = 1.96.

computed_result.critical_surface: xc = 4.19 m, yc = 14.96 m, R = 15.60 m; entry x = -0.22 m (essentially the toe), exit x = 18.67 m on the crest - a toe circle, consistent with Taylor's chart assumption for phi > 0.
computed_result.vs_published: +0.2% vs the ReSSA computer solution (1.9641 vs 1.96), +0.7% vs the graphical chart solution (1.95), +3.4% vs the coarser iterative chart read (1.9). Chart solutions carry ~0.005 read-off precision on Ns, so all three printed values bracket the search result.

report_notes: A clean anchor for chart-vs-computer agreement: the Bishop entry-exit search lands on the toe circle Taylor's chart presumes and matches the manual's own ReSSA computer check to 0.2%. A report should quote the search coverage (entry/exit windows and trial count) and note that stability-chart hand solutions are read-limited to roughly +/-0.05 in FS, which is exactly the spread between the manual's 1.9 iterative and 1.95-1.96 graphical/computer answers. A grid-based circular search with default sampling returned 2.09-2.12 here; the entry-exit search is the appropriate high-resolution verification tool.
source_doc: FHWA NHI-06-088 Soils and Foundations Vol 1.pdf

title: Primary consolidation settlement of a wide embankment on normally consolidated clay (FHWA Soils & Foundations Example 7-3)
source: FHWA NHI-06-088, Soils and Foundations Reference Manual Vol I (Dec 2006), Chapter 7, Example 7-3, printed pp. 7-37 to 7-38
problem: A 'wide' 20-ft (6.096 m) high embankment (fill gamma = 120 pcf = 18.851 kN/m3) is placed over a 10-ft (3.048 m) thick normally consolidated clay layer (gamma = 120 pcf, Cc = 0.5, e0 = 1.0, cv = 0.2 ft2/day) underlain by impervious rock. Because the embankment is wide, the applied stress does not dissipate with depth: delta_p = 20 ft x 120 pcf = 2,400 psf (114.91 kPa) throughout the clay. Initial effective stress at the clay mid-depth: po = 5 ft x 120 pcf = 600 psf (28.73 kPa). Compute the primary consolidation settlement (and, by hand, the time for 90% consolidation with single drainage). Conversions: 1 ft = 0.3048 m, 1 psf = 0.04788 kPa.
published_result: delta_H = H * Cc/(1+e0) * log10((po + delta_p)/po) = 10 ft x (0.5/2) x log10(3,000/600) = 1.75 ft = 21 in (0.53 m). Time for 90% consolidation (Tv = 0.848, single drainage, Hd = 10 ft): t90 = 0.848 x (10 ft)2 / 0.2 ft2/day = 424 days.

computed_result.vs_published: -0.15% vs the printed 1.75 ft (0.5334 m); the layer is treated as normally consolidated (sigma_p omitted, so OCR = 1 and Cr is unused). The t90 = 424 days time-rate check is not part of this dispatch method; hand verification with Tv = 0.848, Hd = 3.048 m and cv = 0.2 ft2/day (0.01858 m2/day) gives 0.848 x 3.048^2 / 0.01858 = 424 days, matching the manual exactly.

report_notes: Textbook single-layer NC consolidation: the module reproduces the manual to 0.15% (the residue is unit rounding). A report should state the wide-fill assumption (no stress dissipation with depth - for narrower fills use a stress-distribution delta_sigma per layer instead of a constant), the NC basis (sigma_p not entered, so the full Cc line is used), and the drainage condition driving the time estimate (single drainage against impervious rock doubles the drainage path versus double drainage, quadrupling t90). Subdividing thick layers reduces the mid-depth-stress approximation error.
source_doc: FHWA NHI-06-088 Soils and Foundations Vol 1.pdf
